summary: In evaluating potential causes of hyperkalemia in pediatric patients, it can be concluded based upon current medical understanding that Cushing’s Syndrome has minimal association due to its primary mechanisms being centered around hormonal imbalances without a known impact on systemic potassium regulation as seen in more common etiologies such as diabetic ketoacidosis or rhabdomyolysis which both affect cellular uptake/export rates influencing plasma K+ concentrations adversely through different pathways involving either increased intracellular shift into cells during glycolytic crises or release from damaged muscle tissues respectively. The unique pathophysiology associated with Cushing’s disease makes it an outlier among likely contributors towards developing elevated blood potassium concentration profiles observed clinically across various age groups including pediatrics; hence, it should logically exclude itself when considering differential diagnoses related solely by presence/absence criteria regarding development of
